name: Sicilian Defense
description: The most popular response to 1.e4 at the top level. Black creates an asymmetric position and fights for counterplay from the start.

moves.commentary: Claim the center.
The Sicilian! They're not mirroring you — they want an asymmetric fight. This leads to the sharpest chess out there.
Develop and prepare to open the center with d4.
They support a future e5 and keep things flexible.
Open it up! You're giving up the d-pawn but you'll get a development lead and open lines.
They take. Now they'll get the half-open c-file — that's their long-term counterplay.
Centralized knight — beautiful. It controls so many squares from here.
They hit your e4 pawn. Time to defend it.
Knight defends e4 and develops. Your setup is coming together.
The Najdorf! This little pawn move does a lot: prevents Bb5, prepares b5 expansion, and keeps options open. Fischer and Kasparov both loved this.
Solid development. You're not committing to an aggressive plan yet — just getting your pieces out safely.
They grab space and kick your knight. They're fighting for the center too.
Retreat the knight. From b3 it supports d4 and watches c5. Not flashy but solid.
They develop and prepare to castle. A rich, double-edged middlegame is coming — welcome to the Najdorf.
Center pawn.
Sicilian Defense.
Develop and prepare d4.
Flexible.
Open the center.
They capture.
Strong centralized knight.
They attack e4.
Defend e4.
The Dragon! They're going to fianchetto the bishop to g7. That bishop on the long diagonal is a BEAST — one of the most dangerous pieces in chess.
Develop and prepare for the Yugoslav Attack — the most aggressive way to fight the Dragon.
There it is — the dragon bishop. It's firing down the whole diagonal. You need a plan to deal with it.
Support e4 and prepare Qd2 + O-O-O. You're setting up to castle queenside and launch a pawn storm at their king.
They castle into the fire. Both sides are about to attack the other's king — it's a race.
Connect rooks and set up Bh6 to trade off that dragon bishop. Once it's gone, their kingside gets weak.
They develop and pressure your d4 knight. Buckle up — this is one of the wildest openings in chess. Both sides attack full speed.
Center.
Sicilian.
Develop.
Flexible setup.
Open the center.
They capture.
Centralize.
They attack e4.
Defend.
The Scheveningen! They build a small but solid pawn wall with d6+e6. Kasparov's favorite structure — flexible and tough to crack.
Develop smoothly. You're keeping your options open — kingside attack or positional play, you'll decide later.
They develop and prepare to castle.
Get your king safe. The real planning starts now.
Both castled. Now it's a chess match.
Grab kingside space! This is the classic plan against the Scheveningen — push f4, maybe f5 later, and squeeze them.
They challenge your central knight. A rich middlegame — you have space, they have solidity. Make your space count.
Center pawn.
Sicilian.
Develop.
Flexible.
Open center.
They take.
Centralize.
Attack e4.
Defend e4.
The Classical — straight development, no fancy pawn moves. They challenge your d4 knight immediately.
Solid development. Prepare to castle and then decide your plan.
They strike the center and kick your knight. Aggressive from them.
Retreat to b3. It's still useful here — covers d4 and c5.
They develop.
Castle and regroup. You have ideas like f4 or Be3 + Nd5.
Both castled. Balanced position with chances for both sides — time to outplay them in the middlegame.
